text:
The replacement is the right move and the proof is essentially correct. The dyadic Girsanov consumes only $\mathbb{E}\int\|F_s\|_H^2 ds$, which is exactly what (3) supplies; the pointwise $V(3/4)$ was always overkill here. Quick verification of the three pieces, then the one place where work is hidden.

**(i)** $\mathbb{E}_s b_n(Z_r(y))b_n(Z_q(z))\le C\rho_{r-s}(y)^{-1/2}\rho_{q-r}(z)^{-1/2}$ is correct by iterated conditioning: given $\mathcal{F}_r$, $V_q(z)-S_{q-r}V_r(z)=\int_r^q S_{q-\sigma}(\cdot,z)dW_\sigma$ has variance $\rho_{q-r}(z)$, and $b_n*g_\sigma$ is Gaussian with variance $\sigma^2+1/n^2\ge\sigma^2$, so $L^\infty$-norm $\le C\sigma^{-1}$ uniformly in $n$. The simplex integral indeed scales like $|t-s|^{3/2}$.

**(ii)** $\rho_\tau^{-1}\le\tau^{-1/2}+d^{-1}$, Hardy on $H_0^1$, and $\|(S_\tau f)'\|_2\le C\tau^{-1/2}\|f\|_2$ combine to give (6). $S_\tau f\in H_0^1$ for $\tau>0$ regardless of $f\in L^2$, so no applicability issue.

**(iii)** The identity $Z^{(s)}_r-Z^{(u)}_r=-S_{r-u}D^n_{s,u}$ is what makes (9) work: the pointwise derivative bound $C\rho_{r-u}(y)^{-1}|h(y)|$ followed by $S_{t-r}$ and (6) gives $C(r-u)^{-1/2}\|D^n_{s,u}\|_H$, which integrates to $C(t-u)^{1/2}$. Tower then gives $\|\mathbb{E}_s\delta A^n\|_{L^2(H)}\le CY_n(h)(t-s)^{5/4}$ and the absorption closes.

**The actual hidden gap is in the passage $K^n\to K$.** "Lower semicontinuity along the ABLM approximation of the mild decomposition" is doing more work than it appears. The uniform $\|D^n_{s,t}\|_{L^2(\Omega;H)}\le C|t-s|^{3/4}$ does **not** pass to $K$ from convergence in distribution alone ŌĆö weak convergence in $L^2(\Omega;H)$ requires uniform $L^p$ bounds for some $p>2$ to upgrade to a $\liminf$ statement on the $L^2$ norm.

**Most economical repair.** Run (8)ŌĆō(9) and the absorption in $L^m$ for $m\in[2,\infty)$. The Gaussian smoothing bound and the Hardy/heat-gradient bound are $L^m$-friendly verbatim, and L├¬'s SSL is stated in $L^m$. This gives $\|D^n_{s,t}\|_{L^m(\Omega;H)}\le C_{m,T}|t-s|^{3/4}$, uniform in $n$. Then the stipulated ABLM Dirichlet extension only has to provide $u^n_t\to u_t$ in probability in $H$ (which is what ABLM actually delivers), and (3) follows by Vitali. I would replace Lemma 1's last sentence with:

> "Steps (8)ŌĆō(9) extend to $L^m$ for every $m\in[2,\infty)$, giving the bound uniformly in $n$ with constant $C_{m,T}$. Together with the stipulated convergence in probability $u^n_t\to u_t$ in $H$, Vitali's theorem yields (3) for $K$."

**Two cleanups worth one sentence each.**

(a) For fixed $n$, $D^n_{s,t}=\mathcal{A}^n_{s,t}$: per-piece error is $\int_{t_i}^{t_{i+1}}\|S_{t-r}[b_n(u^n_r)-b_n(Z^{(t_i)}_r)]\|_H dr$, controlled by $\|b_n'\|_\infty\cdot$(drift increment), vanishing as $|\Pi|\to0$ for each fixed $n$. The blow-up in $n$ is harmless because the identification is per-$n$.

(b) "The Hilbert-valued stochastic sewing lemma of L├¬ in its semigroup form" is underdetermined; the natural reference matching your derivation is ABLM 2024 ┬¦3 (their Proposition 3.8). Cite that and you inherit the semigroup form for free.
